findings.framework: Equality Act 2010 (accessible website as a reasonable adjustment; WCAG 2.2 AA evidence standard)
findings.statutory_citation: Equality Act 2010, s.20-21 (duty to make reasonable adjustments), s.29 (services and public functions: not to discriminate against a person requiring the service)
findings.description: Make reasonable adjustments so disabled users are not put at a substantial disadvantage; WCAG 2.2 AA is the recognised evidence benchmark

penalty.basis: No statutory cap; liability is uncapped compensation for injury to feelings and losses in a county court claim per claimant, plus EHRC action

findings.framework: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (cookie consent and electronic marketing)
findings.statutory_citation: Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003 (SI 2003/2426), reg 6 (storing/accessing information on terminal equipment - cookies); reg 22 (unsolicited electronic mail marketing)
findings.description: Only send electronic marketing with consent or a valid soft opt-in, and give a working unsubscribe

penalty.basis: Historic PECR cap was GBP 500,000; the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 raises PECR maxima toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% turnover). Typical band from ICO PECR penalties.

enforcement.case: ICO v Grocery Delivery E-Services UK Ltd (HelloFresh)
enforcement.summary: Fined for 79m spam emails and 1m spam texts; consent was neither specific nor informed under PECR reg 22.

enforcement.case: ICO cookie compliance review of top 1,000 UK websites
enforcement.summary: ICO reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites for cookie compliance and issuing notices where reject-all is missing or cookies fire before consent.

assessed.framework: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code)
Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (B2C distance selling)
Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading (misleading actions and omissions baseline)
Consumer Rights Act 2015 (fair and transparent terms; goods, services and digital content standards)
Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (fake reviews, drip pricing and false urgency)
Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (changes to UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR)
Equality Act 2010 (accessible website as a reasonable adjustment; WCAG 2.2 AA evidence standard)
UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 (privacy notice, transparency and marketing lawful basis)
ICO data protection fee and register of fee payers (Data Protection (Charges and Information) Regulations 2018)
Modern Slavery Act 2015 s.54 (transparency in supply chains statement)
Online Safety Act 2023 (user-to-user and search services duties)
Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (cookie consent and electronic marketing)
Price and VAT display to consumers (Price Marking Order 2004 and VAT-inclusive pricing)
Company and online trading disclosures (Companies Act 2006 s.82 and Electronic Commerce Regulations 2002 reg 6)

notLegalAdvice: This automated audit is not legal advice; findings are evidence-backed observations for your review and verification with your own advisers.

frameworks.name: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code)
frameworks.regulator: Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) / Committee of Advertising Practice (CAP)
frameworks.citation: UK Code of Non-broadcast Advertising and Direct & Promotional Marketing (CAP Code, 12th edition) - self-regulatory, backed by the CPRs/DMCC via CMA/Trading Standards referral, CAP Code rules 1-3 (misleadingness, substantiation), rule 2 (recognition of marketing communications)

penalty.basis: ASA sanctions are non-monetary (ruling published, ad removal, ad alerts, paid-search removal, referral to Trading Standards/CMA where the CPRs/DMCC then apply monetary penalties)

frameworks.name: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (B2C distance selling)
frameworks.regulator: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
frameworks.citation: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/3134), Sch 2 (pre-contract information for distance contracts); reg 27-38 (14-day cancellation right and model cancellation form); reg 40 (additional payments require active consent)

penalty.basis: Breach can make the contract unenforceable, extend the cancellation period by up to 12 months, and support Trading Standards action; some breaches are criminal offences with a fine

frameworks.name: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading (misleading actions and omissions baseline)
frameworks.regulator: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
frameworks.citation: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1277), now read with the DMCC Act 2024 Part 4, regs 5-6 (misleading actions and omissions), reg 7 (aggressive practices), Sch 1 (banned practices); superseded for practices after 6 April 2025 by DMCC Part 4 Ch 1

penalty.basis: Unlimited fine on conviction and up to 2 years imprisonment for the most serious offences; post-April-2025 conduct enforced under DMCC with direct CMA penalties

frameworks.name: Consumer Rights Act 2015 (fair and transparent terms; goods, services and digital content standards)
frameworks.regulator: Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) / Trading Standards
frameworks.citation: Consumer Rights Act 2015, Part 1 (goods, digital content, services: satisfactory quality, as described, reasonable care); Part 2 (unfair terms; transparency and the grey list at Sch 2)

penalty.basis: Unfair terms are unenforceable against the consumer; CMA/Trading Standards can seek undertakings or court orders and consumers can claim; no fixed statutory fine under Part 2

frameworks.name: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (fake reviews, drip pricing and false urgency)
frameworks.citation: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024, Part 4 Ch 1 (unfair commercial practices) and Sch 20 (banned practices, including fake/concealed-incentive reviews and hidden mandatory fees); commenced by SI 2025/272 on 6 April 2025

penalty.basis: CMA can impose direct penalties up to 10% of global annual turnover for businesses (up to GBP 300,000 for individuals) without going to court

frameworks.name: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (changes to UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR)
frameworks.citation: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, Provisions amending UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR (including PECR penalty uplift and low-risk cookie exemptions); phased commencement by regulations

penalty.basis: DUAA raises the PECR maximum toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% of worldwide turnover); figure applies once the relevant provision is commenced

frameworks.name: Equality Act 2010 (accessible website as a reasonable adjustment; WCAG 2.2 AA evidence standard)
frameworks.citation: Equality Act 2010, s.20-21 (duty to make reasonable adjustments), s.29 (services and public functions: not to discriminate against a person requiring the service)

penalty.basis: No statutory cap; liability is uncapped compensation for injury to feelings and losses in a county court claim per claimant, plus EHRC action

findings.framework: Equality Act 2010 (accessible website as a reasonable adjustment; WCAG 2.2 AA evidence standard)
findings.statutory_citation: Equality Act 2010, s.20-21 (duty to make reasonable adjustments), s.29 (services and public functions: not to discriminate against a person requiring the service)
findings.description: Make reasonable adjustments so disabled users are not put at a substantial disadvantage; WCAG 2.2 AA is the recognised evidence benchmark

penalty.basis: No statutory cap; liability is uncapped compensation for injury to feelings and losses in a county court claim per claimant, plus EHRC action

frameworks.name: UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 (privacy notice, transparency and marketing lawful basis)
frameworks.citation: UK GDPR (assimilated Regulation (EU) 2016/679); Data Protection Act 2018, UK GDPR Arts 5, 6, 13, 14, 21 (transparency, lawful basis, right to object to direct marketing); DPA 2018 Part 2

penalty.basis: Higher maximum of GBP 17.5m or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover (UK GDPR Art 83(5)); typical band from 2025 ICO monetary penalties

frameworks.name: ICO data protection fee and register of fee payers (Data Protection (Charges and Information) Regulations 2018)
frameworks.citation: Data Protection Act 2018 s.137; Data Protection (Charges and Information) Regulations 2018 (SI 2018/480), DPA 2018 s.137-138 (duty to pay charges); SI 2018/480 (fee tiers, register)

penalty.basis: Non-payment attracts a penalty of up to 150% of the highest fee tier (up to about GBP 4,350) under the DPA 2018 fee regime

frameworks.name: Modern Slavery Act 2015 s.54 (transparency in supply chains statement)
frameworks.citation: Modern Slavery Act 2015, s.54 (transparency in supply chains); threshold set by the Modern Slavery Act 2015 (Transparency in Supply Chains) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/1833)

penalty.basis: No financial penalty; the Secretary of State may seek an injunction to compel publication, and non-publication is a reputational and procurement risk

frameworks.name: Online Safety Act 2023 (user-to-user and search services duties)
frameworks.citation: Online Safety Act 2023, Part 3 (duties of care for user-to-user and search services); Part 7 (Ofcom enforcement)

penalty.basis: Ofcom can fine the greater of GBP 18m or 10% of qualifying worldwide revenue, and seek business disruption measures

frameworks.name: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (cookie consent and electronic marketing)
frameworks.citation: Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003 (SI 2003/2426), reg 6 (storing/accessing information on terminal equipment - cookies); reg 22 (unsolicited electronic mail marketing)

penalty.basis: Historic PECR cap was GBP 500,000; the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 raises PECR maxima toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% turnover). Typical band from ICO PECR penalties.

findings.framework: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (cookie consent and electronic marketing)
findings.statutory_citation: Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003 (SI 2003/2426), reg 6 (storing/accessing information on terminal equipment - cookies); reg 22 (unsolicited electronic mail marketing)
findings.description: Only send electronic marketing with consent or a valid soft opt-in, and give a working unsubscribe

penalty.basis: Historic PECR cap was GBP 500,000; the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 raises PECR maxima toward UK GDPR levels (up to GBP 17.5m or 4% turnover). Typical band from ICO PECR penalties.

enforcement.case: ICO v Grocery Delivery E-Services UK Ltd (HelloFresh)
enforcement.summary: Fined for 79m spam emails and 1m spam texts; consent was neither specific nor informed under PECR reg 22.

enforcement.case: ICO cookie compliance review of top 1,000 UK websites
enforcement.summary: ICO reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites for cookie compliance and issuing notices where reject-all is missing or cookies fire before consent.

frameworks.name: Price and VAT display to consumers (Price Marking Order 2004 and VAT-inclusive pricing)
frameworks.regulator: Trading Standards / Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
frameworks.citation: Price Marking Order 2004 (SI 2004/102); Value Added Tax Act 1994; read with CCRs 2013 and DMCC Act 2024 on total price, Price Marking Order 2004 arts 4-7 (selling price and unit price, inclusive of VAT, to consumers)

penalty.basis: Trading Standards can prosecute breaches of the Price Marking Order, typically a fine; DMCC drip-pricing penalties (up to 10% turnover) apply where mandatory fees are hidden

frameworks.name: Company and online trading disclosures (Companies Act 2006 s.82 and Electronic Commerce Regulations 2002 reg 6)
frameworks.regulator: Companies House / local authority Trading Standards
frameworks.citation: Companies Act 2006 s.82; Company, Limited Liability Partnership and Business (Names and Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/17); Electronic Commerce (EC Directive) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2013), CA 2006 s.82-84 and SI 2015/17 regs 24-25 (trading disclosures); SI 2002/2013 reg 6 (information to be provided by an online service provider)

penalty.basis: CA 2006 s.84: a fine on the company and every officer in default at level 3 on the standard scale (up to GBP 1,000); reg 6 breach is actionable and can support enforcement orders

screenedLabel: Screened the catalogue on a limited read of your site

exposureFull.label: median exposure including items under review

exposureNote: Exposure is the midpoint of each statute family's typical enforcement band, de-duplicated to one figure per family; the statutory ceiling shown is the single highest maximum across breached families, never a sum of maxima. Figures follow each statute's own currency and are not converted.

steps.family: privacy and electronic communications (ec directive) regulations 2003 (si 2003/2426)
